prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 5 minutes long when read aloud.

## Goal
Title: Build social confidence and make friends
Description: Help Noah approach peers and join group play on the playground.

## Person
This is for Noah (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

## Therapeutic Focus
Social Initiation Anxiety
Category: SOCIAL
Description: Hesitation to approach peers and join group play; hangs back on the playground.

## Recommendations
- Friendship scripts
- Role-play practice
- Small-group exposure

## Sparkling Moments
- 2024-03-07: Asked a classmate to play and they spent recess together

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Social skills training for shy children in middle childhood" (2022)
   Key findings: Friendship scripts and role-play increased peer initiations in 6-9 year olds
   Therapeutic techniques: Conversation starters; joining-in scripts; brave-body posture; brave-step reward

## Instructions
- Create a complete, flowing therapeutic audio script
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Personalize for Noah (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- Target duration: 5 minutes when read aloud at a calm pace
- Write in English
- Follow the therapeutic audio content structure (warm introduction, understanding the challenge, guided practices, integration)
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
- IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any markdown formatting (no **, ##, *, bullet points, or bold/italic syntax). Write plain spoken prose only, as the script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine
script:
Welcome, Noah. I'm so glad you're here with me today. We're going to talk about something really important: making friends and having fun on the playground. I know it can feel a little tricky sometimes to walk up and join a game. That's okay. Lots of kids feel that way. Today, we're going to practice some super cool skills together to help you feel brave and confident. Are you ready? Let's begin.

First, let's understand what's happening. You know that feeling in your tummy when you see kids playing and you want to join, but your feet feel stuck? That's your brain being a little extra careful, like a superhero checking for safety. It's totally normal. And guess what? You've already done something amazing. Remember that time you asked a classmate to play and you spent the whole recess together? That was fantastic. That shows you can do this. We're just going to build on that brave moment.

Now, let's practice. We're going to start with something called a brave-body posture. Stand up nice and tall where you are. Put your shoulders back, like a strong captain. Plant your feet firmly on the ground. Take a deep breath in... and let it out. Great. This is your brave-body stance. It tells your brain you are ready for adventure.

Next, we'll practice a friendship script. These are just simple words you can use. Let's say them together. You can repeat after me. First, a simple one: "Hi, I'm Noah." Good. Now, let's try one for joining a game. "That game looks fun. Can I play too?" Say it with me: "That game looks fun. Can I play too?" Excellent. You have the words right there in your pocket, ready to use.

Now, let's do a quick role-play in our minds. Close your eyes if you want to. Imagine you're on the playground. You see some kids playing tag. You stand in your brave-body posture. You take a brave step forward. You use your friendly voice and say your script: "That game looks fun. Can I play too?" Imagine them smiling and saying, "Sure!" Imagine running and laughing with them. How does that feel? Pretty good, right? That's you being brave.

So, Noah, here's what to remember. Use your brave-body stance. Remember your friendship scripts like "Can I play too?" And take one brave step at a time. Every time you try, even if it feels a little scary, that's a win. You can celebrate that brave step. Maybe you can tell a grown-up about it later. Speaking of grown-ups, remember you can always ask a parent, teacher, or another adult you trust for a little extra help or to practice with you. They're on your team.

You are brave, you are friendly, and you can do this. I believe in you. Now, go have some fun.
